| | Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw
---
Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18]

Just look at ASML, up 471%... ... and Applied Materials, up 452%... ... and Super Micro Computer, 3,244% surge. And Taiwan Semiconductor, their long-time partner, exploded by 4,744%. Now, NVIDIA is pivoting to a new $1 trillion superproject, poised to shake up the AI industry once again, and they've got a fresh batch of partners. These companies are virtually unknown, even to savvy investors. Want in on the secret? To You Wealth, Martin D. Weiss, PhD Weiss Ratings Founder Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18] | | | First Invеstmеnt Updаtеs brоught tо уоu bу Іnception Media, LLC. ## Remains to be Removed from Cemetery
The National Defense Authorization Act includes a provision sponsored by Sens. John Cornyn and Ted Cruz to [disinter the remains](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkL-OpDAMxp8mdBklJgQoKK6h22uvRB5iIFqHrBLfsPP2J_YVrrP1Sd-f35-P5QMjx3P_jYkm-d7yctJVmUSoLE9kPFcKKe4FJebTurTHTdYFDHTagga3BIz8XlwTpqcJo21osr33Xd_3ZmiOafDQ-9G5zo6mHQZyxq9D62nwW7e5kZo43WYWwFkw1plHP9JgHY5bN4R29aScoRAll4ccJPSNdeN3flF5rDk1PB0iX1W1vxTMCubruh6fFeUWFcz3GgUz5xVZwXxHKZgt3HerYEZm2inoSiUi68_ITKXqQgnjWbVk_aT7yy8Keis56S0X0RWTPvLfKvnU5w8aZL1SIqHyVjA3ZQpfV2SO1ffKmT1h5J--8n-UXxP8CwAA__9Y1ZBR) of Vietnam War veteran and convicted rapist Fernando Cota from the Fort Sam Houston National Cemetery. Cota was the [prime suspect](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkb2unDAQRp8GulmZweCloEhDd9OmRIM93rXinyt7Ltx9-2ijvEHKT0f6pKPz62P_oBBDfvykxKt8-7JnvlpkEa77QZGyZZfCo5KEkgedHsGL3VHhBAMC6t1RiK9d9249lFuGntfBzPNkjFH3_rlqpRDtfZ4mq1HN1ozEhzZ-McNiSY19WN9nA6IeUA1a3czC90HT4qe7G-3MnVbsgpR6kycLf1Pz8VVOrjdbUh_Xp8hn68YfHW4dbtd13X6fX_yGHW5UJdjIHW5vrw43W0P6NyHSBaU6rg0qp3JSBFvyGaywg0qfockbUMgNfKkCjRI8y1eTksFyYuH6As81U3YFbBHqcEMzwjwxEdkJ2C8TaMMHkD8YvDpmNFqPypq-ru7zCjGGNptOq0eiEP86yf-VOFf8EwAA__9lBJ1D) in the murders of six women before he died by suicide in 1984. The son of one of Cota's alleged victims urged Congress to remove his remains from the military cemetery. "_Fort Sam Houston National Cemetery is a sacred resting place for more than 170,000 respected veterans and their loved ones, and it would be a slap in the face to each one of them to allow Fernando Cota, a convicted rapist and alleged serial murderer, to remain buried amongst such heroes_ ,” [said Cornyn](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0LHO4yAQBOCnsbu1gBCICxfXuPuvvdLCZrGRFvgFmzh--9Ofe4SrRxrNfH--li8XKeb9t0s48TuUJePZCJmxLqsjlzf0Ke7VcSxZ6rTHwNuihLqDVKD04l2ka9G9n1bhR9njJK0xd2utePTH5K25GamkQ2vC3YxhRWFXsSrtxy0Y2cfpp0wqpaUSUovBjviQ2o3h_vC3zWCnBfrIpQ58IOPbtUBXeWEdtpJ6mg7m79bdfnVq7tR8nuewlZqvPDTMjnHYy6tT88-tTs3_IlgjEXABfB_PhOCIcEcPDWt0BOlZPVasEGpJEEplaC7BUZ6NS4b8sXAEGyZkrBe0uGf0EDMXIHd2au7r5L_PSBSbsZ0We3KRPoP5_5hfk_obAAD__w4ilW4). The Department of Veterans Affairs has not indicated a timeline for the disinterment process, but Cornyn's office suggested it would be completed within the next year.
## Moving Data Shows Balanced Migration
The number of people moving to Texas this year [roughly equaled](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLGO4yAURb_G7rDgBRu7oNgmXbbd0nqBh4MWTARPcfL3I898wnS3uLpX5_y7rTeMKe7bX8xk-R3KutPREjFTXe-YcHfkc9wqciy70nmLgd0KEkahQIBePcb0WXXv7V36RfVklZmm0Rgj5_5h6X6ZyRHqRZ7RERkKOIJyQcoRQh_tOaYAtAKptBzMQrPSuIRx9hc3Uacl-cilDvwgpje2kD7lRXVwJffJPpifrbv86eDawfU4juFEOIj-n4UOrhmfojEyNYGZanS4N5HLiwQXEWrJ4gdHAchZqr5a_zxiSrFNptNyyxjT9xf_ztDLwlcAAAD__5yIees) the number leaving the state, according to data from moving company Atlas Van Lines. It's the second consecutive year that outbound migration and inbound migration each accounted for 50% of the company's moves in Texas. Between 2019 and 2023, Atlas reported that at least 56% of Texas moves were inbound, with a [high of 57%](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0LGOtCAQB_CnwY6NjCBaUHyN3X7tlWaUwSWHsEFuXd_-osndC1w7-f8n85uP-3hHH3xc_uNKprxdGiPtW6BSKI8TBowz2dUvGYtPUch18a7MI9SguAAOcrTowzHKypqptr2oyAjdtkprXXfVw0yKpsbpqevatmtaJy2h0Er1TsGktKu8OZcJACmgFrK-6Z46IbF3qrPN3BKTNVlfUr6VBxV64-bCkV6Ub3Naq2AepTxZ84_BwGD4fGM85wyGE8JguBoMhjW9fFz4nNYnxoP_mriPHKPl6avw5PgV5ztu_Ed_Bi6vS5lvNKdo-UGYryLPaWcwVNnY5-5D8FurmayXFX247it_--rLwHcAAAD__9fyjHQ) in 2021. "_Whether it's having no income tax that's drawing people in or the political climate and social issues that's pushing them away, these findings underscore the ongoing push-pull dynamic of [Texas's] migration patterns_ ," Atlas said. This year, Arkansas [led the nation](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0D1uwzAMxfHTyFsMiZG_Bg1dvKVrR4OWaIeALAcSGye3L9yhF-j-8Ad-7-s23ZAjp_UTN3LyWvYp0VEiiVCeZoyYPIWN14zCezJ2W3kRP4GG5mLgAnYKyPE92Sq4WYfBVORM17ZN13W6r-7OIGgL2ngA7cNyDX7AGTD0baOHMNuK3RkzANaANlbX3UC9sTgsTR-uviVlNQWWPddyJ6EXliW-9yfl2u9bFd1d5FHU9UPBqGA8jqNGiViemCInKudKwZip7N_ZU1Ew_nkuDzyhqVTZhcfBMXJpO2X1uiHH377875Wng58AAAD__yCCd5Y) with 68% of moves coming from inbound migration, while Louisiana had the highest percentage of outbound moves (66%) for the second year in a row. Overall, fewer Americans are moving than in recent years, a consequence of being locked into low-rate mortgages, according to the report.

Check out this of Austin's famous Trail of Lights. To provide the Texas Flyover staff additional time with their families over Christmas, today's edition and those for the next few days will be a little shorter than usual. We'll be back with a full edition on Monday. Today’s sponsor, , wants to help you find out if you’re overpaying for car insurance by comparing your current coverage in just a few minutes to see if you could put real money back in your pocket. Busy day? Listen to our daily 15-minute podcast while commuting, cooking, or walking the dog—your Flyover fix, anywhere. | | Remains to be Removed from Cemetery The National Defense Authorization Act includes a provision sponsored by Sens. John Cornyn and Ted Cruz to of Vietnam War veteran and convicted rapist Fernando Cota from the Fort Sam Houston National Cemetery. Cota was the in the murders of six women before he died by suicide in 1984. The son of one of Cota's alleged victims urged Congress to remove his remains from the military cemetery. "Fort Sam Houston National Cemetery is a sacred resting place for more than 170,000 respected veterans and their loved ones, and it would be a slap in the face to each one of them to allow Fernando Cota, a convicted rapist and alleged serial murderer, to remain buried amongst such heroes ,” . The Department of Veterans Affairs has not indicated a timeline for the disinterment process, but Cornyn's office suggested it would be completed within the next year. Moving Data Shows Balanced Migration The number of people moving to Texas this year the number leaving the state, according to data from moving company Atlas Van Lines. It's the second consecutive year that outbound migration and inbound migration each accounted for 50% of the company's moves in Texas. Between 2019 and 2023, Atlas reported that at least 56% of Texas moves were inbound, with a in 2021. "Whether it's having no income tax that's drawing people in or the political climate and social issues that's pushing them away, these findings underscore the ongoing push-pull dynamic of [Texas's] migration patterns ," Atlas said. This year, Arkansas with 68% of moves coming from inbound migration, while Louisiana had the highest percentage of outbound moves (66%) for the second year in a row. Overall, fewer Americans are moving than in recent years, a consequence of being locked into low-rate mortgages, according to the report. | Flying together with our sponsor Car insurance premiums are up more than 64% since 2020, and Texas drivers are paying above the national average. () ➤ Austin: Authorities arrested three illegal immigrants from Latvia suspected of operating a high-tech gift card scheme that has defrauded customers of $14 million. () ➤ Dallas: Xavier Mayfield, an 18-year-old South Oak Cliff football player, admitted to accidentally shooting his teammate just hours after their team won the state championship on Saturday, according to police. The unnamed victim is reported to be in stable condition. () ➤ Venus: Controversial social media content creator Ash Trevino, 36, was arrested for alleged healthcare and welfare fraud last week. She was released on a $15,000 bond and has vowed to fight the charges. () ➤ Calhoun County: Conservation groups purchased more than 3,000 acres of coastal wetlands to preserve as a habitat for the area's endangered whooping cranes. name: The Maine Wire
subject: Governor Mills Shuts Down State Offices Ahead of Christmas Eve Storm
body:
Here's the latest breaking news at The Maine Wire

** Governor Mills Shuts Down State Offices Ahead of Christmas Eve Storm (https://mainepolicy.us9.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a975bf67c40a8d8be91ad606d&id=e068026439&e=f093fa355c)
------------------------------------------------------------
By Jon Fetherston on Dec 23, 2025 07:07 pm
Governor Janet Mills has ordered all State of Maine government offices closed Wednesday, December 24, as an overnight storm system is expected to bring hazardous travel conditions across much of the state. The National Weather Service has forecast widespread snowfall for most of Maine and warned that some areas could experience extremely dangerous travel conditions. [...]

Maine’s Department of Health and Human Services has suspended MaineCare payments to Gateway Community Services after a newly completed state audit found the Portland-based nonprofit overbilled the Medicaid program by more than $1 million, sharply escalating a years-long controversy now drawing state, federal, and political scrutiny. According to DHHS, the payment suspension was imposed because [...]

body:
<a clicktracking=off href="http://example.com">
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/EA/EA016/1/99735/images/NPP_Ed_logo_RGB_green1-01.jpg]
Hi Friend,
Welcome to the latest New PA Project Education Fund (NPPEF) newsletter!
Earlier this week, the PA [https://www.politicspa.com/could-changes-finally-be-coming-to-pa-election-laws/141393/] House State Government Committee [https://www.politicspa.com/could-changes-finally-be-coming-to-pa-election-laws/141393/] [https://www.politicspa.com/could-changes-finally-be-coming-to-pa-election-laws/141393/] voted on two bills that have the potential to impact our voting rights: State Rep Mehaffie's Voter ID bill (HB771) and State Rep. McClinton's omnibus bill (HB1396). Both passed out of committee this week and will now be voted on by the full house.
An omnibus bill is a legislative bill that combines multiple, sometimes unrelated, provisions or bills into one single bill. It's essentially a package deal of different legislative proposals, often used to facilitate the passage of legislation, particularly appropriations bills.
HB 771 is sponsored by State Rep. Thomas Mehaffie (Dauphin) and “would require all voters arriving at their election precincts to show identification or attest under penalty of law that they are legally able to vote in Pennsylvania.”
State Rep. Malcolm Kenyatta (Philadelphia) voiced his opposition to the bill. The bill doesn’t address [https://www.politicspa.com/state-government-committee-sends-voter-id-election-omnibus-bills-to-house-floor/141399] the real issues at stake. “We need to solve problems of voter irregularities and voter fraud,” he said. “We do not have demonstrable evidence that leads us to make the point that we need to address voter ID in this way. Let’s talk about the issues that do exist. This legislation doesn’t address those.”
HB 1396 has a more recognizable name behind it – Speaker of the State House Joanna McClinton (Delaware/Philadelphia). State Rep. Ben Waxman (Philadelphia) complemented the Speaker, saying that the legislation is a “comprehensive approach to election reform that tries to dramatically improve the process and makes administration of elections much smoother.”
Among other changes, [https://penncapital-star.com/campaigns-elections/voter-id-and-vote-by-mail-reform-bills-move-to-pa-house] House Bill 1396 would also give election workers up to a week before Election Day to prepare to count mail-in ballots, a process that has been a bottleneck for election results in parts of the state, providing fodder for election deniers.
The vote-by-mail provision has been the subject of numerous lawsuits, including one now before the U.S. Supreme Court, as candidates and parties have argued over how election officials should handle irregularities, such as errors on completed ballots, that are not explicitly addressed.
The bill would make clear that county election officials are required to notify voters if their mail-in ballots have been rejected for the lack of a signature and give the voter an opportunity to “cure” the error.
Upcoming events
As Allegheny County voters prepare for the pivotal 2025 local, municipal and judicial elections, the New PA Project Education Fund (NPPEF) is hosting two critical public candidate forums to give community members the chance to hear directly from the candidates shaping our schools and courts. These moderated discussions will allow voters to ask questions and learn more about each candidate’s priorities and qualifications before heading to the polls.
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/EA/EA016/1/99735/images/Pittsburgh%20School%20board%20forum%208.5x11.png]
Meet the candidates running for the Pittsburgh School Board and hear how they plan to impact our schools and students.
Moderator: Dr. Kimeka Campbell is our Senior Director of Campaigns and Engagement. She is a recognized community activist, political strategist, and civic engagement leader.
With over 15 years of experience in community organizing, political consulting, and nonprofit leadership, Dr. Campbell has been instrumental in shaping voter engagement strategies and building coalitions for progressive change. She co-founded Young Professionals of Color–Greater Harrisburg (YPOC), an organization dedicated to fostering leadership, advocacy, and professional growth for communities of color.
When: Friday, May 9, 2025 | 6:00 PM – 7:30 PM
Where: Hosanna House, 807 Wallace Ave, Wilkinsburg, PA 15221
RSVP [https://click.everyaction.com/k/106793662/542679746/-435660973]
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/EA/EA016/1/99735/images/Allegheny%20judicial%20flyer.png]
Get to know the Allegheny County Common Pleas judicial candidates before you cast your ballot.
Your vote shapes our justice system!
Moderator: Rachel Bracken, Alliance for Justice (AFJ), Senior Regional State Courts Manager
Rachel is a Senior Regional State Courts Manager with Alliance for Justice/AFJ Action. In this position, she manages AFJ/AFJ Action’s involvement in state supreme court justice elections and selection processes. Prior to joining the Justice Team, Rachel was on AFJ’s Outreach Team where she helped create AFJ’s State Court Justice Project and launch AFJ Action’s State Courts Matter initiative. She also coordinated AFJ’s networks of law professors, attorneys, and political donors to advocate around federal judicial nominations and federal courts’ impact on our constitutional rights and freedoms.
When: Monday, May 12, 2025 | 6:00 PM – 8:00 PM
Where: Hosanna House, 807 Wallace Ave, Wilkinsburg, PA 15221
RSVP [https://click.everyaction.com/k/106793661/542679745/229023784]
Social Media of the Week
https://www.instagram.com/p/DJbyV6DPLdJ/ [https://www.instagram.com/p/DJbyV6DPLdJ/]
The deadline to request your mail-in ballot for the primary election is May 13th at 5PM.
Request a mail-in ballot or find your local ballot drop-off location here [vote.pa]
Your county election office must receive your completed mail ballot by 8PM on May 20th.
We recommend mailing it at least 5 days prior, or delivering it by hand at an official ballot drop off location.
#GoVotePA
SHARE ON INSTAGRAM [https://www.instagram.com/p/DJbyV6DPLdJ/]
[https://d15k2d11r6t6rl.cloudfront.net/public/users/Integrators/04eaa6b2-88f4-43b4-b978-7bb0708f5838/7237344C447445787878394747342B75584E47375A57552F745265427662334B544A4A36505A58657A5871474B7A5A6E53705571424758325657365969365747/editor_images/WCM%20logo.JPG] #CourtsMatter
This past Friday, Courts delivered wins for Pennsylvania voters [https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/a-double-win-for-voters-in-pennsylvania] . One ruling ensured that undated or wrongly dated mail-in ballots will be counted, while the other rejected a bid to purge more than 277,000 from the state’s voter rolls.
First, the 3rd U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals denied [https://www.democracydocket.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/445-2025-04-24-Order-denying-PA-Attorney-Generals-motion-to-intervene.pdf] a request by Pennsylvania Attorney General Dave Sunday (R), on behalf of the state, to halt a recent federal court ruling that Pennsylvania must count undated or wrongly dated mail-in ballots.
The ruling stemmed from a lawsuit originally filed [https://www.democracydocket.com/cases/pennsylvania-undated-and-wrongly-dated-mail-in-ballots-challenge-eakin/] in 2022, challenging the state’s instructions to county boards of elections that any mail-in ballot that’s timely cast but either missing a date or containing the wrong date on the outer return envelopes should not be counted.
After the federal judge’s ruling in March of this year, the plaintiffs appealed [https://www.democracydocket.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/441-2025-04-02-Notice-Of-Appeal-.pdf] the decision to the 3rd Circuit. The appeal remains ongoing, but today’s order ensures that counties will count wrongly dated and undated ballots during this month’s primary.
Second, a Pennsylvania district court judge dismissed a lawsuit by the 1789 Foundation to have hundreds of thousands of voters purged from Pennsylvania’s voter rolls.The 1789 Foundation, also known as Citizen_AG, filed [https://www.democracydocket.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/1-2024-10-29-Complaint.pdf] a lawsuit in October against Pennsylvania Secretary of State Al Schmidt (R) claiming Schmidt failed to maintain voter rolls in line with the National Voter Registration Act.
The group wanted the court to force Pennsylvania to remove 277,000 registrants who did not respond to confirmation notices in the 2020 general election cycle removed from the voter rolls.
District Court judge Robert Mariani dismissed [https://www.democracydocket.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/67-2025-05-02-Memorandum-.pdf] the 1789 Foundation’s lawsuit on the grounds that the organization lacked standing to bring the case in the first place.
In law, standing [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standing_(law)] is a condition that a party seeking a legal remedy must show they have, by demonstrating to the court, sufficient connection to and harm from the law or action challenged to support the party's participation in the case.
In other news, this year marks the 15th anniversary of a landmark U.S. Supreme Court ruling that fundamentally reshaped the world of campaign finance. For good-government supporters, it’s a somber milestone. Find out why in Spotlight PA’s full report. [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/05/citizens-united-supreme-court-ruling-campaign-finance-dark-money]
The 2010 decision in Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission , they say, vastly expanded the role of big money in politics and ushered in an era of unprecedented — and in some cases, untraceable — spending on elections by ultra-wealthy special interests.
In Pennsylvania, the court decision’s impact on races for state and local offices was muted — at least early on. That is because of Pennsylvania’s notoriously lax campaign finance laws. Some state lawmakers have sought to tighten rules around so-called “dark money” groups, but their legislation didn’t make it through the full legislature. Continue reading for more on Citizens United and its lasting impact [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/05/citizens-united-supreme-court-ruling-campaign-finance-dark-money] .
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/EA/EA016/1/99735/images/DelCo_2.png] We are hiring!
Let's provide civic education together!
We're looking for people passionate about voting rights in Philadelphia, Allegheny, Erie, Lawrence, Bucks, Montgomery, Chester, and Delaware County to help us shape the future of Pennsylvania.
Join our team and help us provide civic education in our communities and register folks to vote!
Positions start at $20 an hour.
Interested? Contact jobs@nppef.org [jobs@nppef.org]
EMAIL US [jobs@nppef.org]
News You Can Use
There are things every one of us can do [https://www.democracydocket.com/opinion/we-have-more-power-than-trump-wants-us-to-believe-heres-what-you-can-do/] in our daily lives to help ensure free and fair elections. Some actions are small — so small that you might dismiss them as unimportant. Don’t. Every important journey begins with a single step, and the first is no less important than the last.
1. Educate Yourself
Elections can be complicated. The rules vary from state to state — and sometimes even from county to county. These rules also change frequently due to new laws, policies or court rulings. My first recommendation: spend time each month learning what’s happening with voting laws in your state and nationally.
Spotlight PA has an 2025 Election Center [https://www.spotlightpa.org/elections] with all the information you need to be prepared to vote this year, including guides to the big races, key dates, and more.
* New: Which Pittsburgh mayoral candidate most aligns with you? Take our quiz [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/05/pittsburgh-mayor-election-2025-candidates-quiz]
* Get answers to your questions with our AI-powered Election Assistant [https://www.spotlightpa.org/elections/assistant/]
* Everything you need to know about mail voting [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/04/pennsylvania-primary-election-2025-mail-ballot-how-to-request-fill-out-return] , becoming a poll worker [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/04/pennsylvania-primary-election-2025-how-to-be-poll-worker-guide]
* Everything you need to know about how to vote and where to vote [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/05/pennsylvania-primary-election-day-2025-voter-information-registration]
* Guides to Commonwealth, Superior Courts [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/04/pennsylvania-primary-election-2025-commonwealth-superior-court-candidates] , vetting judicial candidates [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/04/pennsylvania-primary-election-2025-candidate-vetting-guide]
* Guides to State College school board [https://www.spotlightpa.org/statecollege/2025/04/pennsylvania-primary-election-2025-state-college-school-board-anderson-huff-barlow-black-desmarais-mcternan-sogor] , Reading City Council president [https://www.spotlightpa.org/berks/2025/04/reading-city-council-president-primary-election-2025]
* Guides to voting as a college student [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/04/pennsylvania-primary-election-2025-college-student-voting-guide]
En Español
* Guía para votar por correo [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/04/pensilvania-elecciones-primarias-2025-votar-correo-como-llenar] , la presidencia del Concejo Municipal de Reading [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/04/reading-concejo-municipal-primarias-elecciones-2025]
* Guía para quién está en la papeleta de las primarias [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/05/pensilvania-primarias-elecciones-2025-votar-informacion] , cómo convertirse en trabajador electoral el 20 de mayo [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/05/pensilvania-elecciones-2025-como-convertirse-trabajador-electoral]
2. Stay Engaged
To protect democracy, we all must stay engaged day in and day out. We can’t just show up right before an election and tune out afterward. If we remain committed, democracy will win.
Check out a Montgomery County Commissioner’s effort to educate voters. It's now an emmy-nominated short film. “Running elections in Pennsylvania's third-most-populous county, Neil Makhija said he’s seen the slippery slope from election misinformation to political violence.” ( Philadelphia Inquirer [https://www.inquirer.com/politics/pennsylvania/montgomery-county-commissioner-neil-makhija-emmy-20250508.html] )
3. Share What You Learn
Once you’ve figured out what’s going on, share that information with your networks and community. It might feel awkward to bring up voter suppression or changes in election laws when we’re not in the middle of a major election — but local elections happen in off years, and special elections are more common than you think. Now is the perfect time to start conversations about voting.
Here, in Pennsylvania, we vote every year, twice a year!
4. Join or Support Pro-Democracy Organizations
Is partisan politics not for you? There are numerous nonprofits and grassroots organizations working every day to protect voting rights and ensure free and fair elections. Supporting these groups with your time or resources helps these groups do their work.
Check out the work New PA Project has been up to lately below. AND , check out our upcoming events listed above!
5. Become a Trained Poll Worker
Many counties face a shortage of poll workers, especially during election season. Being a poll worker requires dedication, attention to detail and a willingness to be trained. It’s a meaningful way to serve your community and help protect the integrity of the voting process.
Here are some resources that may be helpful:
Pennsylvania Capital-Star - Advocacy groups offer training for Pa. primary poll watchers [https://penncapital-star.com/briefs/advocacy-groups-offer-training-for-pa-primary-poll-watchers/]
Spotlight PA - Election 2025: How to become a poll worker for the PA primary [https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/04/pennsylvania-primary-election-2025-how-to-be-poll-worker-guide/]
Franklin County Free Press - Poll Workers Needed for Pennsylvania’s 2025 Primary Election [https://fcfreepresspa.com/poll-workers-needed-for-pennsylvanias-2025-primary-election/]
6. Engage Your Elected Officials
Call and write your elected officials at the federal, state and local levels. Tell them that protecting voting rights and ensuring free and fair elections are your top priorities. Even better, show up at public meetings. Ask them directly what they’re doing to combat voter suppression. Be informed about pending legislation and urge them to support pro-democracy initiatives. This applies no matter where you live or who represents you — Democrat or Republican. Constituent pressure always matters. Don’t let anyone tell you otherwise.
7. Vote in Every Election
Yes — every election. That means local elections, primary elections and special elections. Become a super-voter. The more you vote, the more informed and engaged you become. You’ll also be better positioned to notice and report problems — like changes to polling locations or voting equipment — that could impact turnout or accessibility.
Make a plan to vote in the primary election, Tuesday, May 20th. When are you voting this year? Who are you going with? How are you voting this year -- by mail or on Election Day? As a reminder, the last day to request a mail ballot is Tuesday, May 13th!
Recent Events
[https://d15k2d11r6t6rl.cloudfront.net/public/users/Integrators/04eaa6b2-88f4-43b4-b978-7bb0708f5838/61467144756E73314C4E7335774562454C6644444649732F492B6843473664593068482F7644555678714E564D50363235594F7336424671626839454D43762F/editor_images/491048556_18398781181107182_1677966645695572406_n.jpg] Solidarity for Immigrants
On Monday, May 5th, also Cinco de Mayo, over 300 people, representing 18 community organizations from across Pennsylvania, gathered at the State Capitol in Harrisburg to rally for immigrant rights.
We amplified voices that have been silenced, advocated against injustice, and showed that solidarity is not just a word—it is a force that creates change.
This event was a declaration that Pennsylvania stands against injustice and division.
[https://d15k2d11r6t6rl.cloudfront.net/public/users/Integrators/04eaa6b2-88f4-43b4-b978-7bb0708f5838/61467144756E73314C4E7335774562454C6644444649732F492B6843473664593068482F7644555678714E564D50363235594F7336424671626839454D43762F/editor_images/signal-2025-05-05-120402.jpeg] [https://d15k2d11r6t6rl.cloudfront.net/public/users/Integrators/04eaa6b2-88f4-43b4-b978-7bb0708f5838/61467144756E73314C4E7335774562454C6644444649732F492B6843473664593068482F7644555678714E564D50363235594F7336424671626839454D43762F/editor_images/signal-2025-05-05-122847.jpeg]

[https://d15k2d11r6t6rl.cloudfront.net/public/users/Integrators/04eaa6b2-88f4-43b4-b978-7bb0708f5838/356574676F576F4656484552317542704442666A614C4C4D5334613152463762514A70726B39646D4F494334727651544F3756582B436D446461684F5A536E4F/editor_images/unnamed%20%281%29.jpg] Last Monday, May 5th was the last day to register to vote for the primary election!
Our teams were out in their communities leading up to the voter registration deadline on Monday. We helped folks ensure they have the ability to exercise their right to vote in the primary on Tuesday, May 20th.
Our teams are currently getting ready for GOTV - a time of year where we are on the ground connecting with our communities and mobilizing them to vote.
It's our favorite time of year to build relationships with those we hope to uplift.
Dauphin County

[https://d15k2d11r6t6rl.cloudfront.net/public/users/Integrators/04eaa6b2-88f4-43b4-b978-7bb0708f5838/61467144756E73314C4E7335774562454C6644444649732F492B6843473664593068482F7644555678714E564D50363235594F7336424671626839454D43762F/editor_images/signal-2025-05-03-16402-5_1.jpeg] [https://d15k2d11r6t6rl.cloudfront.net/public/users/Integrators/04eaa6b2-88f4-43b4-b978-7bb0708f5838/61467144756E73314C4E7335774562454C6644444649732F492B6843473664593068482F7644555678714E564D50363235594F7336424671626839454D43762F/editor_images/signal-2025-05-03-164025.jpeg]

Thank you for doing your part to stay informed and engaged!

clean_body: [] Hi Friend, Welcome to the latest New PA Project Education Fund (NPPEF) newsletter! Earlier this week, the PA [] House State Government Committee [] [] voted on two bills that have the potential to impact our voting rights: State Rep Mehaffie's Voter ID bill (HB771) and State Rep. McClinton's omnibus bill (HB1396). Both passed out of committee this week and will now be voted on by the full house. An omnibus bill is a legislative bill that combines multiple, sometimes unrelated, provisions or bills into one single bill. It's essentially a package deal of different legislative proposals, often used to facilitate the passage of legislation, particularly appropriations bills. HB 771 is sponsored by State Rep. Thomas Mehaffie (Dauphin) and “would require all voters arriving at their election precincts to show identification or attest under penalty of law that they are legally able to vote in Pennsylvania.” State Rep. Malcolm Kenyatta (Philadelphia) voiced his opposition to the bill. The bill doesn’t address [] the real issues at stake. “We need to solve problems of voter irregularities and voter fraud,” he said. “We do not have demonstrable evidence that leads us to make the point that we need to address voter ID in this way. Let’s talk about the issues that do exist. This legislation doesn’t address those.” HB 1396 has a more recognizable name behind it – Speaker of the State House Joanna McClinton (Delaware/Philadelphia). State Rep. Ben Waxman (Philadelphia) complemented the Speaker, saying that the legislation is a “comprehensive approach to election reform that tries to dramatically improve the process and makes administration of elections much smoother.” Among other changes, [] House Bill 1396 would also give election workers up to a week before Election Day to prepare to count mail-in ballots, a process that has been a bottleneck for election results in parts of the state, providing fodder for election deniers. The vote-by-mail provision has been the subject of numerous lawsuits, including one now before the U.S. Supreme Court, as candidates and parties have argued over how election officials should handle irregularities, such as errors on completed ballots, that are not explicitly addressed. The bill would make clear that county election officials are required to notify voters if their mail-in ballots have been rejected for the lack of a signature and give the voter an opportunity to “cure” the error. Upcoming events As Allegheny County voters prepare for the pivotal 2025 local, municipal and judicial elections, the New PA Project Education Fund (NPPEF) is hosting two critical public candidate forums to give community members the chance to hear directly from the candidates shaping our schools and courts. These moderated discussions will allow voters to ask questions and learn more about each candidate’s priorities and qualifications before heading to the polls. [] Meet the candidates running for the Pittsburgh School Board and hear how they plan to impact our schools and students. Moderator: Dr. Kimeka Campbell is our Senior Director of Campaigns and Engagement. She is a recognized community activist, political strategist, and civic engagement leader. With over 15 years of experience in community organizing, political consulting, and nonprofit leadership, Dr. Campbell has been instrumental in shaping voter engagement strategies and building coalitions for progressive change. She co-founded Young Professionals of Color–Greater Harrisburg (YPOC), an organization dedicated to fostering leadership, advocacy, and professional growth for communities of color. When: Friday, May 9, 2025 | 6:00 PM – 7:30 PM Where: Hosanna House, 807 Wallace Ave, Wilkinsburg, PA 15221 RSVP [] [] Get to know the Allegheny County Common Pleas judicial candidates before you cast your ballot. Your vote shapes our justice system! Moderator: Rachel Bracken, Alliance for Justice (AFJ), Senior Regional State Courts Manager Rachel is a Senior Regional State Courts Manager with Alliance for Justice/AFJ Action. In this position, she manages AFJ/AFJ Action’s involvement in state supreme court justice elections and selection processes. Prior to joining the Justice Team, Rachel was on AFJ’s Outreach Team where she helped create AFJ’s State Court Justice Project and launch AFJ Action’s State Courts Matter initiative. She also coordinated AFJ’s networks of law professors, attorneys, and political donors to advocate around federal judicial nominations and federal courts’ impact on our constitutional rights and freedoms. When: Monday, May 12, 2025 | 6:00 PM – 8:00 PM Where: Hosanna House, 807 Wallace Ave, Wilkinsburg, PA 15221 RSVP [] Social Media of the Week [] The deadline to request your mail-in ballot for the primary election is May 13th at 5PM. Request a mail-in ballot or find your local ballot drop-off location here [vote.pa] Your county election office must receive your completed mail ballot by 8PM on May 20th. We recommend mailing it at least 5 days prior, or delivering it by hand at an official ballot drop off location. #GoVotePA SHARE ON INSTAGRAM [] [] #CourtsMatter This past Friday, Courts delivered wins for Pennsylvania voters [] . One ruling ensured that undated or wrongly dated mail-in ballots will be counted, while the other rejected a bid to purge more than 277,000 from the state’s voter rolls. First, the 3rd U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals denied [] a request by Pennsylvania Attorney General Dave Sunday (R), on behalf of the state, to halt a recent federal court ruling that Pennsylvania must count undated or wrongly dated mail-in ballots. The ruling stemmed from a lawsuit originally filed [] in 2022, challenging the state’s instructions to county boards of elections that any mail-in ballot that’s timely cast but either missing a date or containing the wrong date on the outer return envelopes should not be counted. After the federal judge’s ruling in March of this year, the plaintiffs appealed [] the decision to the 3rd Circuit. The appeal remains ongoing, but today’s order ensures that counties will count wrongly dated and undated ballots during this month’s primary. Second, a Pennsylvania district court judge dismissed a lawsuit by the 1789 Foundation to have hundreds of thousands of voters purged from Pennsylvania’s voter rolls.The 1789 Foundation, also known as CitizenAG, filed [] a lawsuit in October against Pennsylvania Secretary of State Al Schmidt (R) claiming Schmidt failed to maintain voter rolls in line with the National Voter Registration Act. The group wanted the court to force Pennsylvania to remove 277,000 registrants who did not respond to confirmation notices in the 2020 general election cycle removed from the voter rolls. District Court judge Robert Mariani dismissed [] the 1789 Foundation’s lawsuit on the grounds that the organization lacked standing to bring the case in the first place. In law, standing [)] is a condition that a party seeking a legal remedy must show they have, by demonstrating to the court, sufficient connection to and harm from the law or action challenged to support the party's participation in the case. In other news, this year marks the 15th anniversary of a landmark U.S. Supreme Court ruling that fundamentally reshaped the world of campaign finance. For good-government supporters, it’s a somber milestone. Find out why in Spotlight PA’s full report. [] The 2010 decision in Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission , they say, vastly expanded the role of big money in politics and ushered in an era of unprecedented — and in some cases, untraceable — spending on elections by ultra-wealthy special interests. In Pennsylvania, the court decision’s impact on races for state and local offices was muted — at least early on. That is because of Pennsylvania’s notoriously lax campaign finance laws. Some state lawmakers have sought to tighten rules around so-called “dark money” groups, but their legislation didn’t make it through the full legislature. Continue reading for more on Citizens United and its lasting impact [] . [] We are hiring! Let's provide civic education together! We're looking for people passionate about voting rights in Philadelphia, Allegheny, Erie, Lawrence, Bucks, Montgomery, Chester, and Delaware County to help us shape the future of Pennsylvania. Join our team and help us provide civic education in our communities and register folks to vote! Positions start at $20 an hour. Interested? Contact jobs@nppef.org [jobs@nppef.org] EMAIL US [jobs@nppef.org] News You Can Use There are things every one of us can do [] in our daily lives to help ensure free and fair elections. Some actions are small — so small that you might dismiss them as unimportant. Don’t. Every important journey begins with a single step, and the first is no less important than the last. 1. Educate Yourself Elections can be complicated. The rules vary from state to state — and sometimes even from county to county. These rules also change frequently due to new laws, policies or court rulings. My first recommendation: spend time each month learning what’s happening with voting laws in your state and nationally. Spotlight PA has an 2025 Election Center [] with all the information you need to be prepared to vote this year, including guides to the big races, key dates, and more. New: Which Pittsburgh mayoral candidate most aligns with you? Take our quiz [] Get answers to your questions with our AI-powered Election Assistant [] Everything you need to know about mail voting [] , becoming a poll worker [] Everything you need to know about how to vote and where to vote [] Guides to Commonwealth, Superior Courts [] , vetting judicial candidates [] Guides to State College school board [] , Reading City Council president [] Guides to voting as a college student [] En Español Guía para votar por correo [] , la presidencia del Concejo Municipal de Reading [] * Guía para quién está en la papeleta de las primarias [] , cómo convertirse en trabajador electoral el 20 de mayo [] 2. Stay Engaged To protect democracy, we all must stay engaged day in and day out. We can’t just show up right before an election and tune out afterward. If we remain committed, democracy will win. Check out a Montgomery County Commissioner’s effort to educate voters. It's now an emmy-nominated short film. “Running elections in Pennsylvania's third-most-populous county, Neil Makhija said he’s seen the slippery slope from election misinformation to political violence.” ( Philadelphia Inquirer [] ) 3. Share What You Learn Once you’ve figured out what’s going on, share that information with your networks and community. It might feel awkward to bring up voter suppression or changes in election laws when we’re not in the middle of a major election — but local elections happen in off years, and special elections are more common than you think. Now is the perfect time to start conversations about voting. Here, in Pennsylvania, we vote every year, twice a year! 4. Join or Support Pro-Democracy Organizations Is partisan politics not for you? There are numerous nonprofits and grassroots organizations working every day to protect voting rights and ensure free and fair elections. Supporting these groups with your time or resources helps these groups do their work. Check out the work New PA Project has been up to lately below. AND , check out our upcoming events listed above! 5. Become a Trained Poll Worker Many counties face a shortage of poll workers, especially during election season. Being a poll worker requires dedication, attention to detail and a willingness to be trained. It’s a meaningful way to serve your community and help protect the integrity of the voting process. Here are some resources that may be helpful: Pennsylvania Capital-Star - Advocacy groups offer training for Pa. primary poll watchers [] Spotlight PA - Election 2025: How to become a poll worker for the PA primary [] Franklin County Free Press - Poll Workers Needed for Pennsylvania’s 2025 Primary Election [] 6. Engage Your Elected Officials Call and write your elected officials at the federal, state and local levels. Tell them that protecting voting rights and ensuring free and fair elections are your top priorities. Even better, show up at public meetings. Ask them directly what they’re doing to combat voter suppression. Be informed about pending legislation and urge them to support pro-democracy initiatives. This applies no matter where you live or who represents you — Democrat or Republican. Constituent pressure always matters. Don’t let anyone tell you otherwise. 7. Vote in Every Election Yes — every election. That means local elections, primary elections and special elections. Become a super-voter. The more you vote, the more informed and engaged you become. You’ll also be better positioned to notice and report problems — like changes to polling locations or voting equipment — that could impact turnout or accessibility. Make a plan to vote in the primary election, Tuesday, May 20th. When are you voting this year? Who are you going with? How are you voting this year -- by mail or on Election Day? As a reminder, the last day to request a mail ballot is Tuesday, May 13th! Recent Events [] Solidarity for Immigrants On Monday, May 5th, also Cinco de Mayo, over 300 people, representing 18 community organizations from across Pennsylvania, gathered at the State Capitol in Harrisburg to rally for immigrant rights. We amplified voices that have been silenced, advocated against injustice, and showed that solidarity is not just a word—it is a force that creates change. This event was a declaration that Pennsylvania stands against injustice and division. [] [] [] NPPEF on the Ground [] Last Monday, May 5th was the last day to register to vote for the primary election! Our teams were out in their communities leading up to the voter registration deadline on Monday. We helped folks ensure they have the ability to exercise their right to vote in the primary on Tuesday, May 20th. Our teams are currently getting ready for GOTV - a time of year where we are on the ground connecting with our communities and mobilizing them to vote. It's our favorite time of year to build relationships with those we hope to uplift. Dauphin County [] [] Delaware County [] [] Luzerne County [] [] Super voters vote twice a year, every year ! Thank you for doing your part to stay informed and engaged! [] Last day to register to request a mail ballot: Tuesday, May 13th Primary Election: Tuesday, May 20th Last day to register to vote for the General Election: Monday, October 20th Last day to request mail-in ballot: Tuesday, October 28th General Election: Tuesday, November 4th Important Links: Voter Hotlines: PA Department of State Website [vote.pa.gov] Voter Protection Hotline: 1-866-OUR-VOTE Upcoming elections [] DOS PA Voter Hotline: 1-877-VOTESPA Mail-in and Absentee Ballot [] Spanish/English Hotline: 1-866-VE-Y-VOTA Fact-Checking Election Claims [] Asian Languages Hotline: 1-888-API-VOTE Arabic/English Hotline: 1-844-YALLA-US Paid for by the New PA Project Education Fund New PA Project Education Fund PO Box 453 West Chester, PA 19381 United States If you believe you received this message in error or wish to no longer receive email from us, please unsubscribe: .

# [A Christmas Eve Reflection](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=4399133&post_id=182503829&utm_source=post-email-title&utm_campaign=email-post-title&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgyNTAzODI5LCJpYXQiOjE3NjY1ODQyMTMsImV4cCI6MTc2OTE3NjIxMywiaXNzIjoicHViLTQzOTkxMzMiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.80uNF9u-ZgOSr4TqMS6lQjQHZ4aaeVnA3p2_1Kwvfhs)
### The long arc of small, steady choices creates belonging.
| | [Patrick J. Murphy](https://substack.com/@patrickmurphypa)
---
| Dec 24
---
| [](https://substack.com/@patrickmurphypa)
---

---

| | [READ IN APP](https://open.substack.com/pub/patrickmurphypa/p/a-christmas-eve-reflection?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app)
---

Every year around this time, I find myself thinking about two things: first, the Christmas Eve service in combat back in Baghdad, Iraq, and secondly, the 1946 movie, _It 's a Wonderful Life_.
The film is a classic for its simple truth:

A life's value isn't measured by ambition fulfilled, wealth accumulated, or status achieved, but by the ways we show up for others, especially when no one's watching.
I've always connected to the film on a deeper level, not just because of its powerful message, but also because of its military DNA. Jimmy Stewart wasn't just playing a man pushed to the edge -- he had lived through it. Before making the film, Stewart flew WWII combat missions over Europe as a B-24 bomber pilot. He came home changed. And you can feel that in his performance.
His character George Bailey doesn't get the life he planned. He doesn't travel the world or make a fortune. What he does instead is stay devoted to the people in his community. He helps people buy homes by giving them access to capital. He holds a town together. He shows up again and again, even when it costs him something. And only when he's convinced none of it mattered does he finally get a glimpse of what his life added up to.
That idea has stayed with me -- especially as a parent.

Raising my daughter and son today probably feels different than it did a generation ago. It'll be the greatest thing I'll ever do, especially as the world they're growing up in feels heavier. Faster. More complicated. The list of things parents are supposed to worry about is long and growing.

So on Christmas Eve, I find myself circling one question:
How do you stay centered in a world that doesn't feel very steady itself?
## **1\. Start with the simple stuff -- and tell the truth**
I don't sit my kids down and unload the state of the world on them.
But I don't pretend everything is fine when it isn't either.
You acknowledge reality.
You honor their feelings.
And then you encourage them to take one step forward.
It's not all that different from what the military drilled into me years ago. When everything around you feels overwhelming, you go back to basics. You focus on the next step in front of you.
## **2\. Show Up in the Small Moments**
Kids don't listen the way adults wish they would. They watch. Actions always speak louder than words.
They notice how you talk to the person behind the counter. Whether you're still scrolling when they're trying to tell you something important. How you react when plans fall apart. Whether you own your mistakes or dodge them.
My daughter is learning what respect looks like by watching how I treat people when there's nothing to gain from it.

My son is learning how to move through the world by paying attention to how I handle frustration, pressure, and disappointment.
Kids don't need lectures. They need our example. The same applies to adults.
And the moments that shape them aren't grand gestures -- they're simple things:
How we greet them at the end of a long day.
How we treat the waiter at dinner.
Whether we put the phone down when they speak.
How we apologize when we're wrong.
How we respond when life doesn't go our way.
Leadership is built on small, repeated acts.

Those habits are the things that last.
## **3\. Find Your Anchors**
There's no denying that today's pressures are real:
Rents are rising faster than people's salaries.
Many families can't afford childcare.
Student loans that stretch for decades (I'm still paying mine off 26 years later).
Jobs shaped by AI, automation, and constant reinvention.
A housing market that keeps pushing ownership further out of reach (the average first-time homebuyer is now 40).
Inflation that makes every decision feel heavier.
The emotional load of caring for others while trying to hold yourself and your family together.
A digital world that's constantly diverting your attention.
But the things that steady people haven't changed much at all.
Routines still matter.
Meals together still matter.
Traditions still matter -- even the ones people pretend not to care about.
Honesty. Presence. Community.
Faith for some. A sense of belonging for everyone.

Here's a reminder that you don't need a perfect world -- just a strong foundation.
Resilience.
Kindness.
Discipline.
Courage.
A sense of humor.
And the ability to return to simple things when life gets complicated.
If you truly live these, you're ready for whatever the future holds.
With everything happening in the world, it can feel easier to be cynical than hopeful.
But hope isn't naive.
It's not about ignoring reality.
It's about believing in greater possibilities.
And here's what gives me hope:
My daughter and son will inherit a world filled with challenges, yes -- but also with tools, knowledge, and opportunities unimaginable when I was growing up.
The world has changed a lot over the years.
Fortunately, the things that matter most haven't.
Merry Christmas Eve! While never perfect, it truly is a wonderful life!
**PJM**

clean_body: The long arc of small, steady choices creates belonging. | | ---|---|--- | | | Forwarded this email? ? for more ? Thank you for supporting my work. If my writing resonates with you, consider . Annual paid subscribers in the U.S. will receive a signed copy of my 2008 memoir, (shipping not available to Alaska, Hawaii, and U.S. territories). The long arc of small, steady choices creates belonging. | | | Dec 24 | | | | | | | | | | | | Every year around this time, I find myself thinking about two things: first, the Christmas Eve service in combat back in Baghdad, Iraq, and secondly, the 1946 movie, It 's a Wonderful Life. The film is a classic for its simple truth: A life's value isn't measured by ambition fulfilled, wealth accumulated, or status achieved, but by the ways we show up for others, especially when no one's watching. I've always connected to the film on a deeper level, not just because of its powerful message, but also because of its military DNA. Jimmy Stewart wasn't just playing a man pushed to the edge -- he had lived through it. Before making the film, Stewart flew WWII combat missions over Europe as a B-24 bomber pilot. He came home changed. And you can feel that in his performance. His character George Bailey doesn't get the life he planned. He doesn't travel the world or make a fortune. What he does instead is stay devoted to the people in his community. He helps people buy homes by giving them access to capital. He holds a town together. He shows up again and again, even when it costs him something. And only when he's convinced none of it mattered does he finally get a glimpse of what his life added up to. That idea has stayed with me -- especially as a parent. Raising my daughter and son today probably feels different than it did a generation ago. It'll be the greatest thing I'll ever do, especially as the world they're growing up in feels heavier. Faster. More complicated. The list of things parents are supposed to worry about is long and growing. So on Christmas Eve, I find myself circling one question: How do you stay centered in a world that doesn't feel very steady itself? 1\. Start with the simple stuff -- and tell the truth I don't sit my kids down and unload the state of the world on them. But I don't pretend everything is fine when it isn't either. You acknowledge reality. You honor their feelings. And then you encourage them to take one step forward. It's not all that different from what the military drilled into me years ago. When everything around you feels overwhelming, you go back to basics. You focus on the next step in front of you. 2\. Show Up in the Small Moments Kids don't listen the way adults wish they would. They watch. Actions always speak louder than words. They notice how you talk to the person behind the counter. Whether you're still scrolling when they're trying to tell you something important. How you react when plans fall apart. Whether you own your mistakes or dodge them. My daughter is learning what respect looks like by watching how I treat people when there's nothing to gain from it. My son is learning how to move through the world by paying attention to how I handle frustration, pressure, and disappointment. Kids don't need lectures. They need our example. The same applies to adults. And the moments that shape them aren't grand gestures -- they're simple things: How we greet them at the end of a long day. How we treat the waiter at dinner. Whether we put the phone down when they speak. How we apologize when we're wrong. How we respond when life doesn't go our way. Leadership is built on small, repeated acts. Those habits are the things that last. 3\. Find Your Anchors There's no denying that today's pressures are real: Rents are rising faster than people's salaries. Many families can't afford childcare. Student loans that stretch for decades (I'm still paying mine off 26 years later). Jobs shaped by AI, automation, and constant reinvention. A housing market that keeps pushing ownership further out of reach (the average first-time homebuyer is now 40). Inflation that makes every decision feel heavier. The emotional load of caring for others while trying to hold yourself and your family together. A digital world that's constantly diverting your attention. But the things that steady people haven't changed much at all. Routines still matter. Meals together still matter. Traditions still matter -- even the ones people pretend not to care about. Honesty. Presence. Community. Faith for some. A sense of belonging for everyone. Here's a reminder that you don't need a perfect world -- just a strong foundation. Resilience. Kindness. Discipline. Courage. A sense of humor. And the ability to return to simple things when life gets complicated. If you truly live these, you're ready for whatever the future holds. With everything happening in the world, it can feel easier to be cynical than hopeful. But hope isn't naive. It's not about ignoring reality. It's about believing in greater possibilities. And here's what gives me hope: My daughter and son will inherit a world filled with challenges, yes -- but also with tools, knowledge, and opportunities unimaginable when I was growing up. The world has changed a lot over the years. Fortunately, the things that matter most haven't. Merry Christmas Eve! While never perfect, it truly is a wonderful life! PJM** | | ---|---|--- If my writing resonates with you, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber. subject: Boebert believes God gave her “permission” for fascist rule

body:
She wants a holy war in this country and we need to take away her platform to call for one before it is too late.
| | | | | [](https://t7pkg9hbb.cc.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001hHB4v6buWg4Ev1fJl8ngMRWCTu63FBkVj-A3DNcZ2TCVAIemsU3p1Kp_uNTrC94dP0nXuANSRmOXvq1RyJlrLWNgCVol-SxN5nhDJFJ47cV3qLlJOhncynDAmlS5qqzsl9Ru6eJXT_BEZjkmTFihrL5aMOEZpeC1YsjnCRAPPJUmyFOW0iFfgoN3GL4WZbewcWl2ci40FyP7qBCIjlCQT0g0Bi8NlFoKpsYOHEX_2eU=&c=2d5oLiJfGhPnmhFiI6sZwsmzlvxzTpVts4SFQhJB9CODWlVaa9f3vQ==&ch=tQHnqsn2NN3_He_BeXLTm1KL_SgbbxEeGtkH_9l-Qh5JYeecP6SL4A==)

Peter,
In the months following the Charlie Kirk assassination we have seen an unprecedented power grab and wave of massive escalation from the right.
They are now using Kirk's martyrdom as a way to claim that God is granting them permission to implement theocratic fascist rule by any means necessary and with an iron fist.
And of course, Lauren Boebert is leading the charge and amplifying this rhetoric:

---
| [](https://t7pkg9hbb.cc.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001hHB4v6buWg4Ev1fJl8ngMRWCTu63FBkVj-A3DNcZ2TCVAIemsU3p1Kp_uNTrC94dP0nXuANSRmOXvq1RyJlrLWNgCVol-SxN5nhDJFJ47cV3qLlJOhncynDAmlS5qqzsl9Ru6eJXT_BEZjkmTFihrL5aMOEZpeC1YsjnCRAPPJUmyFOW0iFfgoN3GL4WZbewcWl2ci40FyP7qBCIjlCQTwG-jOtSCsiC0qaup0RIcik=&c=2d5oLiJfGhPnmhFiI6sZwsmzlvxzTpVts4SFQhJB9CODWlVaa9f3vQ==&ch=tQHnqsn2NN3_He_BeXLTm1KL_SgbbxEeGtkH_9l-Qh5JYeecP6SL4A==)
---
Boebert's retweet endorsing far-right pundit Matt Walsh's assertion that the Kirk killing has given the right divine permission to rule as they see fit.
---
Lauren Boebert is a Christian fundamentalist who in the past has said that "God is sending a holy army to save Colorado from the left" -- now her views are becoming mainstream.
Trump has three years left and if this is how bad things are getting we can only imagine what our nation will look like in 2028.
We need to ensure that Boebert does not get another term in Congress where she can continue to push far-right religious extremism. She wants a holy war in this country and we need to take away her platform to call for one before it is too late.
[Trisha Calvarese is the Democrat running to unseat Lauren Boebert and 2026 could be our last chance to beat her before she is able to start the holy war that she is begging for. name: DemsGive
subject: On Christmas Eve we think about what lasts

body:
Strong infrastructure matters
| | | | | [ ](https://ctrk.klclick1.com/l/01KD89ZQ8D9B6ZQQA3W7Q7QVVT_0)
---
| | | | Hey Peter,

Christmas Eve is a moment to pause and reflect on what endures beyond any single election cycle.

Strong Democratic infrastructure does not appear overnight. It is built through sustained organizing, shared resources, and long term investment in candidates and communities that rarely get national attention.

DemsGive exists to help strengthen that foundation by connecting supporters with efforts that are doing the work year round, not just during election season.

If you want to understand how Democratic organizing is evolving and where resources are making the biggest difference, take a few minutes to learn more tonight.

[**Learn More**](https://ctrk.klclick1.com/l/01KD89ZQ8D9B6ZQQA3W7Q7QVVT_1)

– Jesse, DemsGive

name: Freedom First Project
subject: Top Conservative Calls His Diagnosis a 'Death Sentence' - Then Delivers the Christmas Message U.S. Needs
body:
The most important read this Christmas. | [](http://trk.freedomfirstproject._ZD01In0/CUrL26k-_MINkOLXArunGQ)

| | [](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121891186/590929769/1350224769?source=LOC_EM_NOFR_2025.12.24_Happy-Holidays_B1_F1_S1_C1_U1_N-T1&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTIvMS85Mjg3NiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIxMTQ4ODc5My1kMWUwLWYwMTEtODE5NS0wMDBkM2ExZDU4YWEiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=r9dpvgL15dW1xfIM-jRcEKg91fq_hhbyHpOPUumVRNg=) Happy Holidays, friend.

I'm looking forward to quality family time during the holidays, so I wanted to wish you a Merry Christmas and Happy Holidays in advance. ❤️

I hope this season brings you comfort, happiness, and Bright Hope. Oftentimes, it's spending quality time with loved ones that reminds us what's important in life.

For me, that's my two kids (three if you count Roxy, who's posing for a photo with me!), my daughter-in-love, my wonderful granddaughter, family and friends, and you.

Your support has made my year more special than I could have ever imagined. My first year as Delaware's inaugural Black woman Senator has been historic and invigorating, but it's not come without its challenges.

I'm ready to rest up and enjoy the holidays, and I hope you do the same, so together we can come back in 2026 ready to take on MAGA extremism and make waves in the Midterms.

From my family to yours, have a very Merry Christmas and Happy Holidays.

Warmly,

Lisa
---

name: Weston Imer
subject: This Christmas, America prepares to turn 250
disclaimer_text: Paid for by the Team Imer PAC and is not authorized by any candidate or candidates committee
body:
The next chapter of America’s story begins now....
| | | | | [](https://udmlh9hbb.cc.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001yvnjzvOpv1Pj4PWRW4uufYxOUk4B22yzFRVgwkgbTE3ZBN0-eAOWCTy4O9m_ERG6XuGUSqhLCwzjCIhQUR6rEUENLxHMPhHuoPXUpiv-6vjX1-yvMuQPosNavJR4TR_6HYG9p0h8c5uHno9DhxTBZqJ54GR3NsU2G4qY3znCbCl_hP61Zemz24PkX4huq8F6xLLNEbfGH-U=&c=c-mso3zH5cUHzPA5mXOq96JM7sIYpVlOEkDPgby2ul53yicQdgA82w==&ch=O8Mt7qtgijXsdaMvAluw0ekY_bWvGgkiSwOTEfOiYKhqgoq_f8zZmA==)
---
---
| Friend, Christmas is a season of reflection -- a time to slow down, to gather with loved ones, and to consider not just where we are, but how we got here, and what we are called to protect moving forward.
This Christmas carries added significance. In 2026, the United States of America will celebrate her 250th birthday -- a milestone few nations ever reach, and one that reminds us just how rare and extraordinary this country truly is.
For 250 years, America has stood as a testament to a radical idea: that rights come not from kings, governments, or bureaucracies, but from God Himself.
That belief shaped our laws, our culture, and our understanding of freedom. It is why families can gather freely at Christmas, worship openly, speak their minds, and raise their children according to their values.
Those principles were not born out of convenience. They were forged through sacrifice, guided by faith, and defended by generations who understood that liberty, once surrendered, is rarely returned without cost.
That same truth confronts us today.
As we approach America's 250th birthday, the foundations of our nation are being tested. Centralized power continues to grow. Unaccountable institutions demand obedience without consent. And too many leaders are willing to trade principle for comfort, and conviction for convenience.
Team Imer PAC exists because we refuse to accept that future.
Our mission is clear: to recruit, elect, and support America First conservative leaders who understand that freedom requires stewardship, faith, and courage -- not just slogans.
Your Christmas-season support allows us to:
* Identify and prepare principled conservative candidates rooted in faith, responsibility, and limited government
* Build voter registration and grassroots infrastructure in communities the establishment ignores
* Engage young Americans and Gen Z with the timeless truths that built this nation
* Counter establishment Super PACs working to silence grassroots conservatives
* Educate voters on the moral and policy issues that will define the next generation

Christmas reminds us that real change does not come from the top down, but from the heart outward. It is a season rooted in humility, sacrifice, and truth -- values that shaped Western civilization and the American experiment itself.
As we prepare to enter a year that will lead directly into America's 250th birthday, the choices we make now will define the strength of our movement -- and our nation -- for years to come.
🎄 Will you make a special year-end Christmas contribution of $25, $50, $100, or whatever you can give to help close this gap?

clean_body: The next chapter of America’s story begins now.... | | | | | == | Friend, Christmas is a season of reflection -- a time to slow down, to gather with loved ones, and to consider not just where we are, but how we got here, and what we are called to protect moving forward. This Christmas carries added significance. In 2026, the United States of America will celebrate her 250th birthday -- a milestone few nations ever reach, and one that reminds us just how rare and extraordinary this country truly is. For 250 years, America has stood as a testament to a radical idea: that rights come not from kings, governments, or bureaucracies, but from God Himself. That belief shaped our laws, our culture, and our understanding of freedom. It is why families can gather freely at Christmas, worship openly, speak their minds, and raise their children according to their values. Those principles were not born out of convenience. They were forged through sacrifice, guided by faith, and defended by generations who understood that liberty, once surrendered, is rarely returned without cost. That same truth confronts us today. As we approach America's 250th birthday, the foundations of our nation are being tested. Centralized power continues to grow. Unaccountable institutions demand obedience without consent. And too many leaders are willing to trade principle for comfort, and conviction for convenience. Team Imer PAC exists because we refuse to accept that future. Our mission is clear: to recruit, elect, and support America First conservative leaders who understand that freedom requires stewardship, faith, and courage -- not just slogans. Your Christmas-season support allows us to: Identify and prepare principled conservative candidates rooted in faith, responsibility, and limited government Build voter registration and grassroots infrastructure in communities the establishment ignores Engage young Americans and Gen Z with the timeless truths that built this nation Counter establishment Super PACs working to silence grassroots conservatives * Educate voters on the moral and policy issues that will define the next generation Christmas reminds us that real change does not come from the top down, but from the heart outward. It is a season rooted in humility, sacrifice, and truth -- values that shaped Western civilization and the American experiment itself. As we prepare to enter a year that will lead directly into America's 250th birthday, the choices we make now will define the strength of our movement -- and our nation -- for years to come. 🎄 Will you make a special year-end Christmas contribution of $25, $50, $100, or whatever you can give to help close this gap? | | == | | == | | == | | == | | == | We are deeply grateful for your faith, your generosity, and your commitment to this cause. May this Christmas season renew your spirit, strengthen your resolve, and remind us all that while the challenges ahead are real, so too is the hope we carry. | Merry Christmas! | | == | Weston D. body:
Giving up is not an option | | [](https://click.everyaction.com/k/121898633/590953748/1141910609?sourceid=1151060&utm_campaign=26emjge-1222aax-on&utm_medium=ea&utm_source=email&utm_content=vaban&amtOpts=&recurringAmtOpts=&am=&recurringAm=&is_optimized_ask=true&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9DRlJSL0NGUlIvMS85NTk4MiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI3NDAzNGMwZC1kNWUwLWYwMTEtODE5NS0wMDBkM2ExZDU4YWEiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=qnAYNSSvf4LsUkLrVELXND-Q2hhkz04YEalc_YZtJNo=&emci=0fd32c9d-40e0-f011-8195-000d3a1d58aa&emdi=74034c0d-d5e0-f011-8195-000d3a1d58aa&ceid=954237)
